qa_pairs.question: What are the potential risks of using the -O3 optimization option with the GCC compiler, and what alternative is recommended if uncertainty exists?
qa_pairs.answer: The GCC compiler's -O3 option includes optimizations that may be unsafe for certain types of code, particularly those relying on aliasing. If you are unsure about the safety of these optimizations, it is recommended to compile and optimize your code using the -O2 option instead. Alternatively, you can consult the GCC man page (e.g., `man g++`) to identify and disable specific unsafe settings included with -O3.

qa_pairs.question: When linking C++ code with GCC, what issues can arise due to Application Binary Interface (ABI) changes, and how can these issues be resolved when dealing with older precompiled libraries?
qa_pairs.answer: Major ABI changes in GCC, such as the transition from version 4.9 to 5.1, can cause linking to fail if different compiler versions are used for various components, especially when linking against precompiled libraries from vendors. To resolve this, one solution is to recompile all source code, including dependent libraries, using the same compiler version. If recompiling older binaries is not feasible, GCC's Dual ABI feature can be used. For example, with GCC v5.1 or higher, you can pass `-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0` to instruct GCC to link with libraries built using the older ABI.

qa_pairs.question: What precautions should users take regarding default floating-point optimizations when using Intel C/C++ compilers, and what options are recommended for standards-compliant behaviour?
qa_pairs.answer: Intel C/C++ compilers may default to using floating-point optimizations that are potentially unsafe. To ensure ANSI/ISO/IEEE standards-compliant floating-point support, users should consult the Intel man pages (e.g., `man icpc`) and are recommended to use one of two options: `-fp-model precise` or `-fp-model source`.
